next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 18

Kai sat at an outside table at a cafe on the Rue du Grand-Pont, his coffee half-finished and the afternoon showing no interest in moving. Lausanne in August: the lake below the old town catching light between buildings, tourists on the steep sidewalks with shopping bags and good shoes, a woman at the next table reading something on a tablet with the absolute attention of someone who did not want to be disturbed. The cathedral's tower was visible above the rooftops, grey stone against a sky that had been blue all day and looked ready to stay that way until evening.

His wrist ached, the same deep tight ache that arrived every afternoon where the break had sealed too fast. He rotated it once and the ache stayed.

The email from SportsVision was still open on his phone, sitting face-down next to the coffee. It was the third time they'd reached out. They wanted Kai Brennan, dead last in the 200 meters in Doha, on the commentary team for the next Apex Games cycle, explaining the sport to people who liked the spectacle more than the body inside it. He'd be useful now as the athlete who failed in public and kept talking afterward.

He hadn't said no. He hadn't said yes. The not-deciding felt fine, which was new. For most of his career, indecision had been something to clear out before the next training block, the next race, the next heat. Now it was just part of the afternoon, another thing sitting there with the coffee.

A waiter came. Kai ordered another coffee in English and the waiter answered in French and they understood each other fine. The waiter was maybe tw...
open_hooks_to_carry: Lena's 400m freestyle on the patch: slower than her optimization peak, entirely her own, third place
Kai races the 200m unpatched and virus-vulnerable; virus triggers at 160m; he overrides the autonomous firing and finishes last under his own power
The crowd does not understand what they saw; Kai does

payoffs_to_land.description: Kai has learned to feel and override his system's autonomous movements—the drift that was a symptom in Ch 1 becomes a skill in Ch 17
Weeks of practicing manual override pay off: Kai stops his body from running without him and finishes under his own power

prev_tail_excerpt:
...n the morning." He pushed his chair back. "Night, both of you."

"Night."

"Goodnight, Yusuf. And thank you. For the food."

"Thank my mum. I'll tell her someone appreciated it." He raised a hand without turning around and walked out, and the door hissed shut again, and it was Kai and Lena in the empty cafeteria.

The fluorescent strip hummed. The windows were fully dark now. The smell of cumin and saffron and his mother's garam masala still hung in the air, fainter every minute, thinning toward nothing the way smells did when the source was gone and all that was left was what the room had absorbed.

"You all right?" Kai asked.

"Yes." Lena brought her hand down from the back of her neck. "I was thinking about how many meals I've eaten in this room."

"A lot?"

"Since I was sixteen. So." Her eyes moved over the table, the surface wiped clean, the chairs pushed back to where they'd been before any of this. "It won't feel like this again."

Kai looked at Margot's empty chair, then at the folded towel still on the counter by the sink. In the dark window behind Lena there were only the two of them.

"No," Kai said. "Probably not."

Lena stood and came around the table to where he was sitting. At his chair she stopped. He reached for her hand and she gave it to him, his left hand, the one that still complained in the cold, her right hand cool from the water glass. They stayed like that for a while in the empty cafeteria, the fluorescent strip humming above them and the smell of someone's mother's cooking still faintly in the air.

Then they turned off the light and went to pack.
state_deltas_required: Private choices become public performance under global scrutiny; the virus threat is live
Winning and selfhood revealed as different currencies; the characters' choices become visible to the world
